text: Laparoscopic repair of recurrent type IV hiatal hernia after norovigasic bypass. The patient is a sixty-nine-year-old female with a surgical history of a laparoscopic norovigasic bypass and a hiatal hernia repair done in twelve repaired primarily only. She presented at an outpatient clinic with epigastric abdominal pain associated with reflux symptoms for three weeks prior to her presentation. This was worsened with food and fluid intake. Her past medical history is extensive and is listed. Her past surgical history is also significant for a laparoscopic ventricular hernia repair with MASH done in two thousand thirteen. On physical examination, her BMI was two point three. She lost a net of eighty-three pounds since her gastric bypass in two thousand twelve. Her physical examination was unremarkable. Her chest was clear. Her abdominal examination was unremarkable. A CT scan of the abdominal pelvis with contrast was ordered in order to further investigate her abdominal pain. The CT scan reveals herniation of the entire rule limb as well as the gastric pouch in the medias time.

The video shows the herniation of the Roux limb and omentum into the hiatus. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down adhesions between the omentum and the hiatus, with visible assistance from traction and contra-traction. The surgeon lyses adhesions between the small bowel and the peritoneal sac using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection. The peritoneal sac is divided and completely excised using the harmonic scalpel, with the sac being removed from the surgical field.

A flexible endoscope is inserted to delineate the gastric pouch, facilitating the removal of posterior adhesions. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down adhesions between the gastric pouch and pleura, mobilizing the pouch into the abdominal cavity. The hiatal defect is re-approximated using three figure-of-eight sutures with 2-0 Surgidac, applied with an in-the-stitch suturing device, ensuring robust tissue closure without tension.

After completing the posterior cruroplasty, the surgeon reinforces the repair by placing synthetic bio-absorbable mesh, suturing it to the diaphragm with multiple interrupted sutures using the in-the-stitch autosuturing device. Care is taken to avoid injuring vital structures such as the heart, aorta, and IVC. The repair is further reinforced by buttressing a piece of omentum, which is sutured to the diaphragm using a running stitch.

The intraorbital endoscopy reveals a large ulcer within the gastric pouch, with a tablet visible in the crater of the ulcer. The gastric pouch and Roux limb are observed in their intra-abdominal location.
